question: Explain the primary goal of input enhancement in the context of string matching and provide one simple example of this technique.
expectedPoints: The primary goal is to preprocess the pattern string to gather information that can be used to speed up the search in the subsequent text scanning phase.
This avoids checking every possible starting position in the text against the pattern by using the gathered information to make smarter shifts.
An example is building a 'last-occurrence' function for the Boyer-Moore algorithm, which allows for larger, safer shifts when a mismatch occurs.

question: Describe the key idea behind the Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm. How does its failure function work as an input enhancement tool, and what problem does it solve compared to the brute-force approach?
expectedPoints: The key idea is to avoid backtracking in the text string by precomputing the longest proper prefix of the pattern that is also a suffix for every possible starting point of a mismatch (the failure function).

The failure function allows the algorithm, upon a mismatch, to shift the pattern by more than one character by knowing how much of the previous comparison can be reused, thus avoiding redundant comparisons.
It solves the problem of inefficient O(m*n) worst-case time complexity of the brute-force method, achieving O(m+n) time complexity where m is the pattern length and n is the text length.

question: Compare and contrast the input enhancement strategies used in the Boyer-Moore and the Knuth-Morris-Pratt algorithms. Analyze the best-case and worst-case scenarios for each, explaining the heuristics that lead to these performances.
expectedPoints: Boyer-Moore uses two heuristics: the bad-symbol shift (using a last-occurrence table) and the good-suffix shift. It starts comparisons from the end of the pattern, aiming for large skips.

KMP uses a single heuristic: the failure function (prefix-suffix information). It performs comparisons from left to right and shifts the pattern based on the longest self-overlap to avoid restarting from the beginning of the text.

Boyer-Moore's best-case is O(n/m) (e.g., when the pattern's last character never appears in the text, allowing huge shifts). Its worst-case is O(m*n). KMP guarantees a worst-case time complexity of O(m+n) but may not achieve the same best-case speed as Boyer-Moore on practical inputs.
